name: Credit for Increasing Research Activities
eligibility_text: The Credit for Increasing Research Activities under IRC §41 provides a credit to taxpayers who incur qualified research expenses (QREs) in carrying on a trade or business. The credit applies to corporations, S corporations, partnerships, and sole proprietors. Two primary calculation methods exist. Under the regular credit method, the credit equals 20% of QREs exceeding a base amount, which is computed as a fixed-base percentage of the taxpayer's average annual gross receipts for the prior four years, subject to a minimum base amount of 50% of current-year QREs. Alternatively, taxpayers without sufficient historical data, or who elect the Alternative Simplified Credit (ASC), may claim 14% of QREs exceeding 50% of the average QREs for the three preceding tax years (6% if the taxpayer had no QREs in any of those three years). Qualified research expenses include wages paid to employees for qualified services, supplies used in qualified research, and 65% of amounts paid to third-party contractors for qualified research (contract research expenses). Cloud computing costs allocable to qualified research may also qualify under Treasury guidance. Qualified research must satisfy a four-part test: (1) the expense must be for research or experimental expenditures deductible under IRC §174; (2) the research must be undertaken to discover technological information; (3) the research must be intended to be useful in the development of a new or improved business component; and (4) substantially all activities must constitute a process of experimentation. Funded research, research in social sciences, arts, or humanities, and research conducted after commercial production are specifically excluded. Small businesses with gross receipts of $5 million or less and no gross receipts for any tax year before the 5-year period ending with the current tax year may elect under IRC §41(h) to apply up to $250,000 of the credit against payroll taxes (FICA), providing a benefit even in a loss position. Qualified small businesses for payroll-tax offset purposes are those with gross receipts under $5 million for the current year and no gross receipts prior to the 5-year lookback window. The credit is part of the general business credit and is subject to limitations under IRC §38. Unused credits may be carried back one year and forward 20 years. The taxpayer must reduce its §174 deduction (or its basis) by the amount of the credit claimed unless it elects to take a reduced credit under §280C(c). See IRC §41 and Treasury Regulations §1.41-1 through §1.41-8 for full requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 6765 (Credit for Increasing Research Activities)
Contemporaneous project records identifying each business component under development
Employee time-tracking records or affidavits allocating wages to qualified research activities
Payroll records and W-2s for employees performing qualified research
Invoices and contracts for third-party contract research expenses
Supply purchase records tied to qualified research projects
Gross receipts records for base period calculation (regular method) or prior three-year QRE records (ASC method)
Technical documentation describing the process of experimentation for each project
If claiming payroll-tax offset: IRS Form 8974 (Qualified Small Business Payroll Tax Credit for Increasing Research Activities)

name: Credit for Qualified Commercial Clean Vehicles
eligibility_text:
IRC §45W provides a tax credit for qualified commercial clean vehicles placed in service after December 31, 2022, and before January 1, 2033. The credit is available to businesses (including corporations, S corporations, and partnerships) that purchase qualifying vehicles for use in a trade or business.

A 'qualified commercial clean vehicle' is any vehicle that (1) satisfies the requirements of section 30D(d)(1)(C) and (D) (i.e., is manufactured primarily for use on public roads, has a gross vehicle weight rating of no more than 14,500 pounds for light-duty vehicles or more than 14,500 pounds for medium- and heavy-duty vehicles), (2) is propelled to a significant extent by an electric motor drawing from a traction battery with at least 15 kWh of capacity (or 7 kWh for vehicles weighing less than 14,500 lbs), and (3) satisfies the definition of a motor vehicle or mobile machinery.

Credit amounts are calculated as the lesser of: (a) 15% of the vehicle's cost basis (30% if the vehicle is not powered by a gasoline or diesel internal combustion engine), or (b) the incremental cost of the vehicle compared to a functionally equivalent vehicle powered solely by a gasoline or diesel engine. The maximum credit per vehicle is $7,500 for vehicles with a gross vehicle weight rating under 14,500 pounds, and $40,000 for vehicles at or above 14,500 pounds.

Unlike the consumer clean vehicle credit under §30D, §45W has no North American assembly requirement and no MSRP cap, making it accessible to a broader range of commercial fleet purchasers. There is also no modified adjusted gross income limitation, as the credit is claimed by business entities rather than individuals.

The credit is part of the general business credit under §38 and is subject to the passive activity and at-risk rules. Any unused credit may be carried back one year and carried forward 20 years under the general business credit rules of §39. The credit reduces the taxpayer's basis in the vehicle by the amount of the credit claimed.

Eligible businesses must retain purchase documentation, vehicle identification numbers, manufacturer certifications of battery capacity, and records demonstrating business use. The IRS has issued guidance (Notice 2023-9 and Notice 2024-5) addressing eligibility, the incremental cost safe harbors, and manufacturer certification procedures. Taxpayers should consult these notices and IRC §45W for complete requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8936
Vehicle purchase invoice or bill of sale
Vehicle identification number (VIN) records
Manufacturer certification of battery capacity (kWh)
Incremental cost documentation or IRS safe harbor reference (Notice 2023-9, Notice 2024-5)
Records demonstrating business use of the vehicle
Depreciation schedules reflecting basis reduction for credit claimed

name: Credit for Production of Clean Hydrogen
eligibility_text:
IRC §45V provides a production tax credit for the production of clean hydrogen at a qualified clean hydrogen production facility. The credit applies to hydrogen produced after December 31, 2022, at a facility placed in service after that date, and sold or used by the taxpayer in a trade or business. The credit is available for a 10-year period beginning on the date the facility is originally placed in service.

The credit amount is determined by the lifecycle greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions rate of the hydrogen produced, measured in kilograms of CO2 equivalent per kilogram of hydrogen (kg CO2e/kg H2). The base credit rate per kilogram of clean hydrogen is scaled as follows: hydrogen with a lifecycle GHG emissions rate of not more than 0.45 kg CO2e/kg H2 qualifies for the maximum base credit of $0.60/kg; hydrogen with an emissions rate between 0.45 and 1.5 kg CO2e/kg H2 receives $0.20/kg; between 1.5 and 2.5 kg CO2e/kg H2 receives $0.15/kg; and between 2.5 and 4.0 kg CO2e/kg H2 receives $0.12/kg. These base rates are multiplied by five (up to $3.00/kg at the highest tier) if the facility meets prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements under IRC §45V(e). Facilities that begin construction before the 60-day period after Treasury issues relevant guidance may qualify for the bonus rates without satisfying prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements if construction commenced before that date.

A 'qualified clean hydrogen production facility' means a facility that produces hydrogen through a process that results in a lifecycle GHG emissions rate of no greater than 4 kg CO2e/kg H2. Lifecycle GHG emissions are determined using the most recent GREET model (Greenhouse Gases, Regulated Emissions, and Energy Use in Technologies) as modified and published by the Secretary of Energy, or another methodology approved by the Secretary. Taxpayers must obtain a verification from a qualified, unrelated third party confirming the lifecycle emissions rate and the amount of hydrogen produced.

Taxpayers may elect to treat the §45V credit as a direct payment (elective pay) under IRC §6417, or may transfer the credit to an unrelated party under IRC §6418. The credit is subject to recapture if the facility ceases to meet requirements. Hydrogen must be produced in the United States or a U.S. possession. The credit is reduced if investment tax credits under §48 or §48A are also claimed with respect to the same facility. See IRC §45V and IRS Notice 2023-29, Notice 2024-13, and proposed Treasury regulations for full requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 7210
Third-party verification report of lifecycle GHG emissions rate
Documentation of hydrogen production volumes (kg)
Records of hydrogen sale or use in a qualifying trade or business
Prevailing wage and apprenticeship compliance records (if claiming bonus credit rate)
Facility placed-in-service documentation
GREET model lifecycle analysis or approved alternative methodology documentation
Construction commencement records
Records supporting elective pay or credit transfer election, if applicable

name: Clean Fuel Production Credit
eligibility_text:
Under IRC §45Z, the Clean Fuel Production Credit is available to taxpayers who produce and sell clean transportation fuel in the United States. The credit applies to fuel produced and sold after December 31, 2024, through December 31, 2027. Both corporate and pass-through (partnership, S corporation, sole proprietorship) entities may claim this credit.

Qualifying fuel must be a 'clean fuel' as defined under §45Z, meaning transportation fuel with an emissions rate that does not exceed 50 kilograms of CO2e per million British thermal units (mmBTU). The credit amount varies based on the emissions profile of the fuel: for non-aviation transportation fuel, the base credit is $0.20 per gallon (or gallon-equivalent), scaling up to $1.00 per gallon if the fuel meets the prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements under §45Z(a)(1). For sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), the base credit is $0.35 per gallon, scaling up to $1.75 per gallon with prevailing wage and apprenticeship compliance.

The credit rate is further adjusted by an 'emissions factor' that reflects the lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions of the fuel, as determined using methodologies approved by the Secretary of the Treasury in consultation with the Secretary of Energy and the Administrator of the EPA. Fuels with lower lifecycle emissions receive proportionally higher credit rates. Producers must use a qualifying emissions measurement methodology, such as those consistent with GREET (Greenhouse gases, Regulated Emissions, and Energy use in Transportation) or other approved models.

To be eligible, the taxpayer must (1) produce the qualifying fuel at a facility in the United States, (2) sell the fuel to an unrelated party for use as transportation fuel, and (3) register with the IRS as a clean fuel producer. Fuel blending alone generally does not qualify; the taxpayer must be the producer of the fuel. The credit is not available for fuel derived from co-processing with petroleum feedstocks, unless specific rules permit.

Prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements under §45Z(a)(2) must be met during the construction and alteration of the production facility to receive the enhanced (5x) credit rate. If these requirements are not satisfied, only the base credit rate applies.

The credit is part of the broader clean energy incentive framework established by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022. Producers should consult Treasury guidance and IRS Notice 2024-49 (and subsequent notices) for detailed lifecycle emissions methodologies and registration requirements. See IRC §45Z for full requirements, limitations, and phase-down rules.
documentation_required: Records of fuel production volume (gallons or gallon-equivalents)
Documentation of fuel type and feedstock sources
Lifecycle emissions analysis using IRS/Treasury-approved methodology (e.g., GREET model output)
IRS clean fuel producer registration confirmation
Records of sale to unrelated parties with evidence of use as transportation fuel
Prevailing wage and apprenticeship compliance records (if claiming enhanced credit rate)
Contracts and invoices related to fuel sales
Facility location and ownership documentation
Annual production and sales logs
IRS Form 7213 and supporting schedules

name: Credits for Electricity Production from Renewable Resources
eligibility_text:
IRC §45 provides a production tax credit (PTC) for electricity produced from qualified energy resources and sold to unrelated persons during the 10-year period beginning on the date the qualifying facility is placed in service. Qualified energy resources include wind, closed-loop biomass, open-loop biomass, geothermal energy, solar energy, small irrigation power, municipal solid waste, qualified hydropower, and marine and hydrokinetic renewable energy. The facility must be owned by the taxpayer and the electricity must be produced in the United States or U.S. possessions.

For facilities placed in service after December 31, 2021, the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 restructured §45. The base credit rate is 0.3 cents per kilowatt-hour (kWh) for wind, closed-loop biomass, and geothermal energy, and 0.15 cents per kWh for other qualified resources. If the facility meets prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements, the credit is multiplied by five, yielding 1.5 cents per kWh (or 0.75 cents per kWh for other resources), adjusted annually for inflation. The IRS annually publishes the inflation-adjusted credit amounts; for 2024, the base amounts are approximately 0.33 cents per kWh and 0.165 cents per kWh respectively, with the bonus rates at approximately 1.64 cents and 0.82 cents per kWh.

Bonus credit amounts are available for facilities that satisfy the domestic content requirement (additional 10% of the otherwise applicable credit amount) or that are located in an energy community (additional 10%). Facilities with a maximum net output of less than 5 MW (AC) may also qualify for the low-output bonus.

The credit is claimed on IRS Form 8835 and reported on the taxpayer's income tax return. For pass-through entities (partnerships, S corporations), the credit flows through to partners or shareholders. The PTC is a general business credit subject to the limitations of IRC §38 and carryback/carryforward provisions of IRC §39 (1-year carryback, 20-year carryforward). The credit may be reduced if the taxpayer receives certain other federal subsidies or tax-exempt financing for the facility.

Under IRC §45(b)(9), taxpayers may elect to transfer the credit to an unrelated third party (transferability) or, for eligible taxpayers, elect a direct pay option under IRC §6417. Small and mid-sized businesses that own qualifying wind, solar, geothermal, or biomass generation facilities — including farmers with wind turbines or biogas projects — are the most common SMB claimants. The 10-year credit period, combined with prevailing wage bonuses and domestic content adders, makes this a significant long-term tax benefit. See IRC §45 and related Treasury regulations for full requirements and phase-out rules.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8835 (Renewable Electricity Production Credit)
Records of kilowatt-hours produced and metered
Facility placed-in-service date documentation
Power purchase agreements or sales records to unrelated purchasers
Prevailing wage and apprenticeship compliance records (if claiming 5x multiplier)
Domestic content certifications (if claiming bonus)
Energy community designation documentation (if applicable)
Construction and ownership records for the qualifying facility
Any federal grant or tax-exempt financing records

name: Clean Electricity Production Credit
eligibility_text:
The Clean Electricity Production Credit under IRC §45Y is a technology-neutral production credit enacted by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, effective for facilities placed in service after December 31, 2024 (replacing the legacy §45 PTC for post-2024 facilities). It applies to corporations, individuals, and pass-through entities that own qualifying clean electricity generation facilities.

To qualify, a facility must produce electricity from a source with a lifecycle greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions rate of not greater than zero grams of CO2-equivalent per kilowatt-hour, as determined by the Secretary of Energy using a lifecycle analysis methodology. Qualifying energy sources generally include wind, solar, geothermal, hydropower, nuclear, and certain other zero-emission technologies meeting the GHG threshold. The credit applies to electricity produced and sold to an unrelated party during the 10-year period beginning on the date the facility is placed in service.

Credit Amount: The base credit rate is 0.3 cents per kilowatt-hour (kWh) of clean electricity produced and sold. For facilities meeting prevailing wage and apprenticeship (PWA) requirements under §45Y(g), the credit rate is multiplied by five, yielding 1.5 cents per kWh. Bonus credits are available for facilities located in energy communities (as defined under §45(b)(11)(B)) or for use of domestic content, each potentially adding additional percentage multipliers per Treasury guidance.

The credit is part of the §48E investment credit regime when elected under the 'elective payment' or 'transferability' provisions enacted by the IRA (§6417 and §6418), which allow certain eligible entities, including tax-exempt organizations and governmental entities, to receive direct cash payments, and allow other taxpayers to transfer the credit to third parties.

For small and mid-sized businesses (SMBs) such as community solar developers, agricultural wind projects, or small hydropower operators, typical credit claims vary significantly based on facility size and annual output. A modest 1 MW solar facility producing roughly 1,500 MWh per year at the full 1.5 cents/kWh PWA rate would generate approximately $22,500 annually, scaling upward substantially for larger or multi-facility operations.

The credit phases out when the annual greenhouse gas emissions from the U.S. electricity sector fall below 25% of 2022 levels, or by a fixed date as may be established by Treasury. Detailed facility certification, GHG emissions rate determinations, and PWA compliance documentation are required. See IRC §45Y and Treasury Reg. guidance for full requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 3468 (investment credit, if applicable)
Records of electricity produced and sold (metered kWh output)
Facility placed-in-service documentation
Lifecycle GHG emissions rate certification from the Secretary of Energy
Prevailing wage payroll records (if claiming 5x multiplier)
Apprenticeship compliance records (if claiming 5x multiplier)
Unrelated-party sales contracts or documentation
Domestic content certifications (if claiming bonus credit)
Energy community designation documentation (if applicable)
IRS Form 3800 (General Business Credit)

name: Credit for Carbon Oxide Sequestration
eligibility_text:
IRC §45Q provides a credit for carbon oxide captured from an industrial facility or direct air capture facility and disposed of in secure geological storage, used as a tertiary injectant in enhanced oil or natural gas recovery, or utilized in a manner that results in permanent sequestration. The credit applies to qualified carbon oxide captured after the facility's placed-in-service date and only if construction begins before January 1, 2033 (as extended by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022).

Credit rates vary by capture method and disposition. For qualified carbon oxide captured and geologically stored (not EOR), the rate is $85 per metric ton (indexed for inflation starting in 2027). For carbon oxide used in enhanced oil or gas recovery and geologically stored, the rate is $60 per metric ton. For direct air capture (DAC) facilities, rates are higher: $180 per metric ton for geological storage and $130 per metric ton for EOR use. These rates apply to facilities beginning construction after 2022; prior facilities have lower statutory rates.

To qualify, the industrial facility must capture at least 12,500 metric tons of qualified carbon oxide per year (or 1,000 metric tons per year for electricity generating facilities with capacity of 75 MW or less). Direct air capture facilities must capture at least 1,000 metric tons per year. The taxpayer must have paid for or incurred costs for capturing and disposing of the carbon oxide, or have had the carbon oxide transported and injected into a geological formation.

The credit is available for 12 years from the date a carbon capture equipment unit is placed in service. Taxpayers may elect, under IRC §45Q(f)(3)(B), to transfer the credit to the person that physically or contractually ensures the secure geological storage, such as the entity that disposes of the captured carbon oxide. The credit may also be transferred or sold under the IRA's transferability provisions.

A prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirement applies to facilities beginning construction after December 31, 2022; failure to meet these requirements reduces the credit to a base rate of one-fifth of the standard rate. Safe harbors and correction mechanisms are provided in IRS guidance.

The credit is claimed on IRS Form 8933 and attached to the taxpayer's federal income tax return. Detailed reporting of metric tons captured, method of disposal, and verification via third-party monitoring, reporting, and verification (MRV) plans consistent with EPA Subpart RR or Subpart UU are required by Treasury Regulations. See IRC §45Q and associated Treasury Regulations and IRS Notice 2020-12 for full requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8933
EPA Subpart RR or Subpart UU monitoring, reporting, and verification (MRV) plan
Third-party verification reports of metric tons captured and sequestered
Construction commencement documentation (5% safe harbor or continuous construction records)
Facility placed-in-service records
Prevailing wage and apprenticeship compliance records (for post-2022 construction)
Contracts or agreements with geological storage operators
Annual capture and disposition tonnage records
Election statement if credit is transferred to storage/disposal party

name: Energy Credit (Investment Tax Credit for Energy Property)
eligibility_text:
IRC §48 provides an investment tax credit (ITC) for qualifying energy property placed in service during the tax year. Originally enacted in the Energy Tax Act of 1978 and significantly expanded by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (IRA), the credit applies to both corporate and individual/passthrough taxpayers who own qualifying energy property.

Qualifying energy property under §48 includes: (1) solar energy property (equipment that uses solar energy to generate electricity, heat/cool a structure, or provide hot water); (2) fiber-optic solar illumination property; (3) geothermal energy property; (4) qualified fuel cell property; (5) qualified microturbine property; (6) combined heat and power (CHP) system property; (7) qualified small wind energy property; (8) waste energy recovery property; (9) electrochromic glass property; (10) energy storage technology (added by IRA, effective 2023); (11) qualified biogas property; (12) microgrid controllers; and (13) linear generators. Interconnection property may also qualify for certain smaller systems.

Base credit rates vary by property type. For most solar and storage projects, the base ITC is 6% of the tax basis (qualifying investment). A bonus rate of 30% applies if the project meets prevailing wage and apprenticeship (PWA) requirements under §48(a)(10), or if the project has a maximum net output below 1 MW (the small project exception under §48(a)(9)). Additional bonus credits of 10% each are available for domestic content (using U.S.-manufactured steel, iron, and manufactured products) and for siting in an energy community (areas affected by fossil fuel industry decline). Projects in low-income communities may qualify for an additional 10–20% adder under the §48(e) Low-Income Communities Bonus Credit Program, which is subject to annual capacity allocations.

The credit is calculated on the taxpayer's basis in the qualifying energy property. A taxpayer who claims the full 30% ITC must reduce the depreciable basis of the property by 50% of the credit claimed (§50(c)). The credit is part of the general business credit and is generally subject to the tax liability limitation, though unused credits may be carried back one year and forward up to 20 years. Notably, for qualifying projects, the credit may be transferred to unrelated parties (§6418, added by IRA) or, for eligible entities, received as a direct payment (elective pay under §6417).

For SMBs, solar installations on commercial property are the most common qualifying investment. A typical 100 kW commercial rooftop solar system costing $150,000–$200,000 installed would generate a credit of $45,000–$60,000 at the 30% rate (assuming PWA or small-project eligibility). Larger projects with storage, geothermal, or wind components can generate substantially higher credits. See IRC §48 and IRS Form 3468 instructions for full property definitions and applicable percentages.
documentation_required: IRS Form 3468 (Investment Credit)
IRS Form 3800 (General Business Credit)
Signed construction/installation contracts
Paid invoices and receipts for energy property
Evidence of placed-in-service date (commissioning report, utility interconnection agreement)
Engineer or contractor certification of system specifications
Prevailing wage and apprenticeship compliance records (if claiming 30% bonus rate)
Domestic content certification (if claiming domestic content bonus)
Energy community documentation (if claiming energy community bonus)
Depreciation schedule showing basis reduction (§50(c))
Property ownership records or lease documentation

name: Clean Electricity Investment Tax Credit
eligibility_text:
IRC §48E, enacted by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, provides a technology-neutral Clean Electricity Investment Tax Credit for qualifying clean electricity facilities and energy storage technology placed in service after December 31, 2024. The credit replaces the legacy IRC §48 ITC and §45 PTC framework for facilities placed in service after that date, extending incentives to any electricity generation facility with a greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions rate of zero or less as determined by the Secretary of Energy.

Qualifying facilities include solar, wind, geothermal, hydropower, nuclear, and any other zero-emissions electricity generation technology. Qualifying energy storage technology includes stand-alone battery storage and other systems regardless of the generation source they serve, provided they meet technical requirements under the statute.

Base Credit Rate: The base credit is 6% of the qualified investment (basis) in the qualifying facility or energy storage property. The credit increases to 30% of qualified investment if prevailing wage and apprenticeship (PWA) requirements are satisfied under IRC §48E(d). Facilities with a maximum net output of less than 1 megawatt (MW) are automatically deemed to satisfy PWA requirements and qualify for the 30% rate.

Bonus Credits: Additional bonus credits of up to 10 percentage points are available for facilities located in energy communities (areas with historical fossil fuel employment or a closed coal mine or plant) under §48E(f). A domestic content bonus of up to 10 percentage points applies if the facility meets steel, iron, and manufactured products requirements aligned with §45Y(g)(11). Low-income community bonuses of 10% or 20% are available for qualifying facilities under the §48E(h) low-income bonus credit program, subject to annual capacity allocations administered by the IRS and Department of Energy.

The total credit rate can reach up to 50% of qualified investment when all applicable bonuses are stacked.

For small and medium businesses, the credit applies to the full eligible basis of the facility. Partnerships and S-corporations may pass the credit through to partners and shareholders. The credit is subject to recapture if the property is disposed of or ceases to qualify within five years of being placed in service.

The credit may be transferred to unrelated third parties under IRC §6418 or claimed as a direct payment (elective pay) by certain tax-exempt and governmental entities under IRC §6417. Taxpayers electing transfer or direct pay must register with the IRS prior to filing. See IRS Notice 2023-29, Notice 2024-30, and related guidance for energy community definitions and bonus credit procedures. See IRC §48E for full requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 3468 (Investment Credit)
Cost basis records and placed-in-service documentation for qualifying facility or storage property
Engineering or certification records establishing zero greenhouse gas emissions rate
Prevailing wage payroll records and certified payroll reports (if claiming 30% rate via PWA compliance)
Apprenticeship program records and hours logs (if claiming 30% rate via PWA compliance)
Domestic content manufacturer certifications (if claiming domestic content bonus)
IRS energy community eligibility documentation (if claiming energy community bonus)
IRS low-income bonus credit allocation letter (if applicable)
IRS pre-filing registration number (if electing credit transfer under §6418 or direct pay under §6417)
Depreciation schedules and asset purchase agreements

name: Credit for Alternative Fuel Vehicle Refueling Property
eligibility_text:
IRC §30C provides a tax credit for the cost of qualified alternative fuel vehicle refueling property placed in service during the taxable year. The credit was significantly modified and extended by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (IRA), effective for property placed in service after December 31, 2022, and before January 1, 2033.

For business property (depreciable property used in a trade or business), the credit equals 6% of the cost of each item of qualified refueling property, with a maximum credit of $100,000 per item of property. The credit rate increases to 30% per item if prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements under §30C(g) are satisfied, with the same $100,000 per-item cap.

For non-business (personal use) property, the credit equals 30% of the cost, capped at $1,000 per item.

Qualified alternative fuel vehicle refueling property includes equipment used to store or dispense alternative fuels (such as electricity, natural gas, propane, hydrogen, E85, or biodiesel blends of at least 20%) into the fuel tank of a motor vehicle propelled by such fuel, or to recharge electric vehicles. Property must be original use property (original use commencing with the taxpayer) and must be placed in service in the United States.

Beginning with tax years after December 31, 2022, the IRA added a critical geographic restriction: qualified property must be located in an eligible census tract, defined as either a low-income community (as defined under §45D(e)) or a non-urban area. Taxpayers must verify census tract eligibility using IRS or Treasury mapping tools.

The credit is part of the general business credit under §38 for business property and is subject to passive activity and at-risk rules. Unused general business credits may be carried back one year and forward twenty years. The basis of the qualifying property must be reduced by the amount of the credit claimed under §30C(e).

For individuals claiming the non-business credit, the credit is a personal credit subject to the limitations of §26. The credit is non-refundable for personal use property.

SMBs commonly install EV charging stations or alternative fuel dispensers at business locations. A small business installing multiple charging units at a commercial facility, each costing $15,000–$50,000, could claim credits ranging from $900 (6% of $15,000) up to $100,000 per item if prevailing wage requirements are met. See IRC §30C and IRS Form 8911 instructions for full requirements and eligible census tract verification.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8911 (Alternative Fuel Vehicle Refueling Property Credit)
Receipts and invoices for purchase and installation of refueling property
Documentation of property placed-in-service date
Evidence of eligible census tract location (Treasury/IRS mapping tool output)
Certification that property is original-use
Records of prevailing wage and apprenticeship compliance if claiming 30% rate
Depreciation records reflecting basis reduction for credit amount
Contractor certifications or permits if applicable

name: Credit for Construction of Energy-Efficient New Homes
eligibility_text:
Under IRC §45L, eligible contractors who construct or manufacture qualified new energy-efficient homes or dwelling units located in the United States may claim a tax credit per qualifying unit sold or leased to another person for use as a residence. The credit was significantly restructured by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (IRA) for homes acquired after December 31, 2022.

For homes acquired after December 31, 2022, the credit amount depends on the type of dwelling and the applicable energy standard met:

1. Single-family homes: A credit of $2,500 is available for homes that meet the Energy Star Single-Family New Homes program requirements. A credit of $5,000 is available for homes certified as Zero Energy Ready under the Department of Energy's Zero Energy Ready Home (ZERH) program.

2. Multifamily dwelling units: A credit of $500 per unit is available for units meeting Energy Star Multifamily New Construction program requirements, or $1,000 per unit for units meeting the DOE Zero Energy Ready Home program requirements. These amounts increase to $2,500 and $5,000 per unit respectively if prevailing wage requirements are satisfied during construction.

For homes acquired before January 1, 2023 (prior law), the credit was $1,000 or $2,000 per unit depending on whether the home met 30% or 50% energy-savings targets compared to a reference standard (generally the 2006 IECC), with manufactured homes eligible for the $1,000 tier.

The eligible contractor is defined as the person who owns the qualifying residence at the time of construction and originally sells or leases it for use as a residence. Speculative builders, general contractors who own the lots, and developers who construct and sell completed homes generally qualify. The home must be a dwelling unit located in the United States, and the basis of the unit must be eligible for depreciation (i.e., held for sale in the ordinary course of business).

The credit is part of the general business credit and is subject to the passive activity rules and at-risk rules as applicable. Unused credits may be carried back one year and forward up to 20 years under the general business credit carryover rules.

Key requirements include obtaining a certification from a qualified third-party verifier (e.g., an accredited HERS rater or Energy Star verifier) documenting that the home meets the applicable energy standard. The contractor must retain this certification. See IRC §45L and IRS Notice 2023-65 (and related guidance) for full certification and substantiation requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8908 (Energy Efficient Home Credit)
Third-party energy certification from qualified certifier (e.g., HERS rater, Energy Star verifier)
Energy Star or DOE Zero Energy Ready Home program certification documents
Settlement statements or closing documents showing date of sale or lease
Records establishing eligible contractor status (ownership of home during construction)
Cost basis records for each qualifying unit
Prevailing wage payroll records (for multifamily units claiming enhanced credit amounts)

name: Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit
eligibility_text:
IRC §45X provides a production tax credit for eligible components manufactured in the United States and sold to unrelated parties. The credit was enacted by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 and applies to eligible components produced and sold after December 31, 2022. Qualifying taxpayers include corporations, partnerships, S corporations, and sole proprietors engaged in domestic manufacturing of eligible components.

Eligible components and their credit amounts are defined in IRC §45X(b) and include: (1) Solar energy components — including photovoltaic cells ($0.04/Wdc), solar modules ($0.12/Wdc), inverters (ranging from $0.02 to $0.065/Wdc by type), and solar-grade polysilicon ($3/kg); (2) Wind energy components — including blades ($0.02/Watt of rated capacity), nacelles ($0.05/Watt), towers ($0.03/Watt), and offshore wind foundation components ($0.02/Watt); (3) Inverters and power electronics; (4) Battery components — including electrode active materials ($35/kWh), battery cells ($35/kWh), and battery modules ($10/kWh for modules without cells, $45/kWh for modules with cells); and (5) Critical minerals — credit equals 10% of the production costs of qualifying critical minerals as defined by reference to 30 USC §1606(a).

For solar and wind components, the credit phases out beginning in 2030 and is eliminated after 2032 (IRC §45X(b)(3)). Battery and critical mineral credits do not phase out under current law.

The credit is claimed per unit of eligible component produced and sold in an arm's-length transaction to an unrelated third party during the taxable year. There is no per-taxpayer cap, but the credit is limited to production that occurs within the United States (including U.S. territories in some cases). The credit is generally nonrefundable but may be transferred or received as a direct payment (elective pay) for certain tax-exempt entities and governmental entities under IRC §6417. Taxable entities may transfer the credit to unrelated parties under IRC §6418.

Related-party sales are excluded; a sale to a person with more than 50% common ownership does not qualify unless an election is made and applicable rules are satisfied. Taxpayers must maintain detailed production and sales records to substantiate credit amounts. See IRC §45X and IRS Notice 2023-29 and final regulations under Treasury Decision 10005 for full requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 7207
Production logs showing units manufactured and dates
Sales invoices to unrelated parties
Bills of materials and cost records for critical minerals
Facility location documentation establishing U.S. manufacture
Records of component specifications (wattage, kWh capacity, weight as applicable)
Related-party analysis supporting arm's-length sales
Transfer agreement documentation if credit is transferred under IRC §6418
Elective pay election documentation if applicable under IRC §6417

name: Advanced Manufacturing Investment Credit
eligibility_text:
IRC §48D provides an Advanced Manufacturing Investment Credit equal to 25% of the qualified investment in any advanced manufacturing facility placed in service after December 31, 2022. The credit was created by the CHIPS and Science Act of 2022 to incentivize domestic production of semiconductors and semiconductor manufacturing equipment.

Qualified Property: To claim the credit, a taxpayer must place in service qualified property that is integral to an advanced manufacturing facility. Qualified property includes tangible property subject to depreciation or amortization, used in the manufacturing of semiconductors or semiconductor manufacturing equipment. The property must be used in a facility where the primary purpose is the manufacturing of semiconductors or semiconductor manufacturing equipment.

Advanced Manufacturing Facility: An advanced manufacturing facility is defined as a facility with the primary purpose of manufacturing semiconductors or semiconductor manufacturing equipment. This includes facilities engaged in the production of integrated circuits, memory chips, photomasks, and the specialized equipment used in those manufacturing processes.

Credit Rate: The base credit rate is 25% of the qualified investment. Unlike certain other energy credits, the §48D credit does not provide a separate bonus for meeting prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements—the 25% rate is the statutory rate. However, the credit is reduced if the taxpayer receives certain federal subsidies or grants from the CHIPS Act for the same facility.

Direct Pay and Transferability: Under §48D(d), eligible taxpayers—including tax-exempt entities, state and local governments, and certain other entities—may elect to treat the credit as a direct payment (elective pay) rather than a nonrefundable credit against tax liability. Additionally, taxpayers may elect to transfer all or a portion of the credit to an unrelated third party under the rules of §6418, subject to IRS registration requirements and anti-abuse rules.

Recapture: The credit is subject to recapture if, within 10 years of the facility being placed in service, the taxpayer engages in certain prohibited transactions, including expanding semiconductor manufacturing capacity in a foreign country of concern, entering into certain joint ventures or technology licensing agreements with foreign entities of concern, or engaging in certain material expansions with such entities.

SMB Applicability: While the §48D credit was primarily designed for large semiconductor manufacturers, it is available to any size business investing in qualifying property. Smaller semiconductor equipment manufacturers, specialty chip fabricators, and photomask producers may qualify. The credit is claimed on IRS Form 3468 (Investment Credit). See IRC §48D and associated Treasury regulations for full requirements, including the elective pay election process and recapture provisions.
documentation_required: IRS Form 3468 (Investment Credit)
Records establishing placed-in-service date of qualified property
Evidence that facility's primary purpose is semiconductor or semiconductor equipment manufacturing
Cost basis records for qualified property
Documentation of any federal CHIPS Act grants or subsidies received (for credit reduction calculation)
IRS pre-filing registration for elective pay or transferability elections
Evidence of compliance with prohibited transaction restrictions for recapture avoidance
Depreciation schedules for qualified property

name: Zero Emission Nuclear Power Production Credit
eligibility_text:
IRC §45U provides a production tax credit for electricity produced at a qualified nuclear power facility. The credit was enacted under the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (IRA) and applies to electricity produced and sold after December 31, 2023, at facilities with a nuclear reactor licensed by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC).

The base credit amount is 0.3 cents per kilowatt-hour (kWh) of electricity produced and sold to an unrelated party. If the taxpayer meets prevailing wage requirements under IRC §45U(b)(2), the credit is increased to 1.5 cents per kWh (adjusted for inflation after 2024). The credit phases down if the facility's gross receipts from electricity sales exceed a threshold tied to electricity market prices. Specifically, a phase-out applies when the annual gross receipts rate (the effective electricity price) exceeds 2.5 cents per kWh, with the credit fully phased out at 4.5 cents per kWh (both figures are also subject to annual inflation adjustment).

A 'qualified nuclear power facility' is defined as any nuclear facility that (1) uses nuclear energy to produce electricity, (2) is licensed by the NRC, (3) is not a facility for which a credit under IRC §45 (the general production tax credit) or §48C is allowed for the same taxable year, and (4) began production before enactment of the IRA (i.e., existing facilities). New advanced nuclear facilities that qualify under §45J or §45 are generally not eligible for §45U simultaneously.

The credit is available for taxable years beginning after December 31, 2023, and applies to electricity sold through December 31, 2032. The credit is fully transferable and eligible for direct pay (elective payment) by tax-exempt entities, state and local governments, Indian tribal governments, the Tennessee Valley Authority, and rural electric cooperatives under IRC §6417.

For passthrough entities and individual owners, the credit flows through on Schedule K-1. Corporations claim it directly. Because the credit is calculated per kWh of electricity sold, smaller operators, cooperatives, or partial owners of nuclear facilities will see proportionally smaller credits. Large nuclear operators with multiple reactor units may generate credits in the tens or hundreds of millions of dollars annually. Typical SMB-scale claims (e.g., minority interest owners or small cooperative participants) may range from a few thousand dollars to several million depending on ownership share and facility output. See IRC §45U and IRS Notice 2023-29 for full requirements and inflation adjustment tables.
documentation_required: Records of electricity produced (kWh meter readings or independent engineer reports)
Evidence that electricity was sold to an unrelated third party
NRC operating license for the nuclear facility
Payroll and wage records demonstrating prevailing wage compliance if claiming enhanced 1.5¢/kWh credit
Records of annual gross receipts from electricity sales for phase-out calculation
IRS Form 8911 or applicable credit form (confirm current form with IRS)
Schedule K-1 documentation for passthrough allocations
Elective payment election forms under IRC §6417 if applicable

name: Credit for Low-Income Housing
eligibility_text:
Under IRC §42, the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) provides a dollar-for-dollar federal income tax credit to owners and investors in qualified low-income residential rental housing projects. The credit is computed annually over a 10-year credit period and is allocated by state housing credit agencies, each of which receives a per-capita allocation of credit authority from the IRS.

To qualify, a building must be a qualified low-income housing project in which the owner elects one of two minimum set-aside tests: (1) at least 20% of the residential units are rent-restricted and occupied by tenants whose income does not exceed 50% of the area median gross income (AMGI), or (2) at least 40% of units are rent-restricted and occupied by tenants at or below 60% of AMGI. Additional income-averaging elections are available under the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2018 (minimum 40% of units averaging at or below 60% of AMGI).

Two credit rates apply: the '9% credit' (applicable percentage set so that the present value of 10 years of credits equals approximately 70% of qualified basis) applies to new construction or substantial rehabilitation not federally subsidized; the '4% credit' (approximately 30% present value) applies to federally subsidized new construction and to the acquisition cost of existing buildings undergoing substantial rehabilitation. Since 2020, the 9% rate is a minimum floor; the 4% rate was also set to a 4% minimum floor under the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2021.

Qualified basis is the portion of a building's eligible basis attributable to low-income units. Eligible basis generally includes construction and rehabilitation costs but excludes land. A basis boost of up to 130% of eligible basis is available for buildings in Difficult Development Areas (DDAs) or Qualified Census Tracts (QCTs) designated by HUD.

Tax credits are allocated by state agencies via a competitive application process governed by each state's Qualified Allocation Plan (QAP). Investors (typically limited partners or LLC members) claim the annual credit on IRS Form 8586 (Low-Income Housing Credit) and attach IRS Form 8609-A (Annual Statement for Low-Income Housing Credit), which is issued by the state agency. Pass-through entities report the credit on Schedule K-1.

The credit is subject to recapture under IRC §42(j) if a qualified low-income building fails to meet occupancy requirements during the 15-year compliance period. Extended use agreements typically require an additional 15-year compliance period (30 years total).

For SMB-scale developers or pass-through investors, annual credit claims commonly range from $50,000 to several million dollars depending on project size, eligible basis, and applicable credit percentage. See IRC §42 and IRS Notice 2022-52 (applicable percentages) for full requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8586 (Low-Income Housing Credit)
IRS Form 8609 issued by state housing credit agency
IRS Form 8609-A (Annual Statement for Low-Income Housing Credit)
Schedule K-1 from pass-through entity
Qualified allocation plan application and award documents
Extended use agreement / land use restriction agreement (LURA)
Tenant income certifications (Form 8823 if noncompliance)
Rent rolls and lease records demonstrating set-aside compliance
Eligible basis and qualified basis calculations
Construction cost certifications and audited financial statements
IRS Form 3800 (General Business Credit) if applicable

name: Credit for Employer-Paid FICA Taxes on Tips
eligibility_text:
Under IRC §45B, employers may claim a general business credit equal to the employer's share of FICA taxes (Social Security and Medicare taxes under IRC §3111) paid on tips received by employees in connection with food or beverage service where tipping is customary. The credit applies to both corporations and pass-through entities (S corporations, partnerships, sole proprietors).

To qualify, three conditions must be met: (1) the employer must operate a food or beverage establishment; (2) employees must receive tips from customers in connection with providing, delivering, or serving food or beverages; and (3) the tipping must be customary. The credit does not apply to tips used to meet the federal minimum wage rate in effect on January 1, 2007 ($5.15 per hour), meaning only FICA taxes on tips above that threshold generate the credit. This floor applies even if the actual federal minimum wage later increased.

The credit amount equals the employer's share of FICA taxes (6.2% Social Security + 1.45% Medicare = 7.65% combined employer rate) multiplied by the excess of total tips over the minimum-wage threshold amount. For example, if a tipped employee earns $30,000 in tips and the minimum wage offset is $10,000, the employer may claim a credit on $20,000 in excess tips: $20,000 × 7.65% = $1,530 for that employee.

Employers must actually pay the FICA taxes on the tips to claim the credit. The credit claimed under §45B reduces the employer's deduction for FICA taxes paid under IRC §164(a) by the amount of the credit. Alternatively, an employer may elect to forgo the credit and take a full deduction for FICA taxes paid.

The §45B credit is a component of the general business credit under IRC §38 and is subject to the general business credit ordering and carryback/carryforward rules (1-year carryback, 20-year carryforward). Passive activity limitation rules may apply to pass-through owners.

Employers report the credit on IRS Form 8846 (Credit for Employer Social Security and Medicare Taxes Paid on Certain Employee Tips) and attach it to the annual income tax return. The credit is available to any size employer in the food and beverage industry with tipped employees; there is no employee count or gross receipts cap in the statute. See IRC §45B and related Treasury guidance for full requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8846
Employee tip records (Form 4070 or employer equivalent)
Payroll records showing FICA taxes paid on tips
W-2 forms for tipped employees
Records of hours worked and wages paid to tipped employees
Employer's quarterly payroll tax returns (Form 941)

name: Credit for Rehabilitation of Historic Structures
filing_deadline: with annual return for the year the rehabilitated building is placed in service
eligibility_text: Under IRC §47, taxpayers may claim a federal income tax credit equal to 20% of qualified rehabilitation expenditures (QREs) incurred in the certified rehabilitation of a certified historic structure. A separate 10% credit that previously applied to pre-1936 non-historic buildings was repealed by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 for expenditures incurred after December 31, 2017 (subject to transition rules). To qualify, the building must be a 'certified historic structure,' meaning it is listed individually on the National Register of Historic Places or is located in a registered historic district and certified by the Secretary of the Interior as contributing to the significance of that district. The rehabilitation must be a 'certified rehabilitation,' meaning it is certified by the Secretary of the Interior as consistent with the historic character of the property. QREs generally include amounts properly includible in computing the basis of the building (not the land) that relate to the rehabilitation, not new construction or enlargement. QREs do not include acquisition costs, parking facilities, or expenditures for tax-exempt use property in certain circumstances. The TCJA (P.L. 115-97) modified the credit so that it must be claimed ratably over a 5-year period (20% per year) beginning in the tax year the rehabilitated building is placed in service, rather than entirely in the placed-in-service year. The credit is subject to basis reduction: the taxpayer must reduce the depreciable basis of the building by the amount of the credit claimed. For pass-through entities such as partnerships and S corporations, the credit flows through to partners or shareholders. Passive activity rules under IRC §469 may limit the ability of individual investors to use the credit in the year earned unless they meet the active participation or real estate professional tests; unused credits carry forward. There is no statutory minimum expenditure floor expressed as a dollar amount for historic structures (the old substantial rehabilitation test required QREs to exceed the greater of $5,000 or adjusted basis over a 24-month measurement period; see IRC §47(c)(1)(C) and related Treasury regulations for current requirements). The National Park Service and State Historic Preservation Offices administer the certification process. Taxpayers must obtain Part 1, Part 2, and Part 3 approvals from the NPS before and during the project. See IRC §47 and Treasury Regulation §1.48-12 for full requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 3468 (Investment Credit)
National Park Service Part 1 certification (Historic Structure evaluation)
National Park Service Part 2 certification (Description of Rehabilitation)
National Park Service Part 3 certification (Request for Certification of Completed Work)
State Historic Preservation Office approvals
Detailed records of all qualified rehabilitation expenditures
Construction contracts and invoices
Architect and engineer certifications
Depreciation schedules showing basis reduction
Pass-through Schedule K-1 if credit flows from partnership or S corporation

name: Railroad Track Maintenance Credit
eligibility_text:
Under IRC §45G, eligible taxpayers may claim a credit equal to 50% of qualified railroad track maintenance expenditures (QRTMEs) paid or incurred during the taxable year. The credit was originally enacted in 2004 and has been extended multiple times; taxpayers should verify current-year applicability, as it has periodically lapsed and been reinstated.

Eligible taxpayers are Class II or Class III railroads (as defined by the Surface Transportation Board) and any person who transports property using the rail facilities of such a railroad, or who furnishes railroad-related property or services to such a railroad. Class I railroads are generally excluded.

Qualified railroad track maintenance expenditures are amounts paid or incurred for maintaining railroad track (including roadbed, bridges, and related track structures) owned or leased by a Class II or Class III railroad as of January 1, 2005. The expenditure must be for track located in the United States.

The credit is subject to a per-mile limitation. The maximum credit per mile is capped based on the number of miles of railroad track assigned to the taxpayer. Specifically, the aggregate credit available to an eligible taxpayer and all persons assigned miles by the railroad cannot exceed the product of $3,500 multiplied by the number of miles of track assigned to the eligible taxpayer for the taxable year. This per-mile cap is a critical limiting factor for smaller regional railroads and shippers.

For taxpayers other than the railroad itself (i.e., shippers or service providers), the Class II or Class III railroad must assign track miles to them, and the total miles assigned by the railroad plus miles retained by the railroad cannot exceed the railroad's total track miles. Proper assignment agreements should be documented and retained.

The credit is nonrefundable and is claimed against regular tax liability. Unused credits may be subject to general business credit carryback (1 year) and carryforward (20 years) rules under IRC §38 and §39. The amount of QRTMEs that give rise to the credit must be reduced by the credit amount (basis reduction requirement).

For small to mid-size Class II or Class III railroads or qualifying shippers with meaningful track assignments, the credit can represent a significant reduction in federal tax liability. Taxpayers should maintain detailed records of all qualifying expenditures by track segment, executed mile-assignment agreements, and documentation confirming Class II or Class III status of the railroad. See IRC §45G for full requirements and current phase-in or expiration provisions.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8900 (Railroad Track Maintenance Credit)
Records of qualified railroad track maintenance expenditures by track segment
Documentation of Class II or Class III railroad status (Surface Transportation Board classification)
Track mile assignment agreements (for non-railroad claimants)
Invoices and contracts for maintenance work performed
Depreciation and basis records for track assets
Proof of U.S. location of maintained track

name: New Markets Tax Credit
eligibility_text: Under IRC §45D, the New Markets Tax Credit (NMTC) is available to taxpayers—including corporations, individuals, and pass-through entities—who make a qualified equity investment (QEI) in a Community Development Entity (CDE) that has been allocated NMTC authority by the CDFI Fund. The credit equals 39% of the QEI, claimed over a 7-year credit period: 5% in each of the first three years and 6% in each of the remaining four years. The aggregate credit on a $1 million QEI therefore equals $390,000. CDEs must use substantially all of the QEI proceeds (generally at least 85%) to make qualified low-income community investments (QLICIs) in qualified active low-income community businesses (QALICBs) located in low-income communities—census tracts with poverty rates of at least 20% or median family income at or below 80% of the applicable area median. A QUALICB must derive less than 15% of its revenues from certain excluded activities (e.g., rental of residential property, golf courses, massage parlors, gambling facilities). CDEs must receive a NMTC allocation from the Treasury's CDFI Fund through a competitive application process; the annual allocation cap has typically been set by Congress (most recently at $5 billion per year for 2020–2025 under the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021). Investors do not directly apply to the IRS for NMTC authority; rather, they invest in CDEs that hold allocations. To claim the credit, taxpayers file IRS Form 8874 and must maintain their investment for the full 7-year compliance period or risk recapture. Recapture can occur if the CDE ceases to be a CDE, redeems or repurchases the QEI, or fails to use substantially all proceeds as required under §45D(g). For SMB investors participating through pass-through vehicles, typical QEI amounts range from roughly $1 million to $10 million, generating credits of $390,000 to $3.9 million over seven years. Smaller participations through leveraged loan structures may yield effective SMB-level credit claims in the $50,000–$500,000 range. The credit is subject to the general business credit limitations of IRC §38 and may be carried back 1 year and forward 20 years. See IRC §45D and Treasury Reg. §1.45D-1 for full requirements.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8874
CDFI Fund allocation agreement for the CDE
Qualified equity investment certificate from the CDE
CDE organizational documents confirming CDE status
Evidence of low-income community designation (census tract data)
QLICI loan or investment agreements
Annual compliance certifications for the 7-year period
Records supporting substantially-all test (85% deployment of proceeds)
Purchase price and closing documents for the QEI

name: Tax Credit for Contributions to Scholarship Granting Organizations
eligibility_text:
The federal tax credit for contributions to scholarship granting organizations (SGOs) is a relatively recent federal incentive that allows individual taxpayers and passthrough entity owners (S corporation shareholders, partnership partners, and sole proprietors) to claim a federal income tax credit for qualifying charitable contributions made to eligible scholarship granting organizations. SGOs are nonprofit entities that provide private school scholarships or tuition assistance to qualifying students, typically from lower- or middle-income families.

Under the relevant IRC framework, contributions to qualifying SGOs may generate a federal credit in addition to, or in lieu of, the standard charitable deduction under IRC §170. The credit is structured as a percentage of the qualifying contribution amount. Based on legislative proposals reflected in JCT scoring, the credit rate is generally expected to be set at or near 100% of the contribution, subject to annual caps and income-based phase-outs applicable to individual filers.

To qualify, the recipient organization must be recognized as a tax-exempt entity under IRC §501(c)(3) and must operate primarily to provide scholarships for tuition at eligible elementary or secondary schools. Contributions may be in cash or appreciated property, subject to standard substantiation rules under IRC §170(f).

For passthrough entities, the credit flows through to individual owners on Schedule K-1 and is claimed at the individual level. Taxpayers must reduce any corresponding charitable deduction under IRC §170 by the amount of the federal credit claimed, to prevent a double tax benefit.

Annual credit amounts are subject to per-taxpayer caps, which vary based on filing status and adjusted gross income. Individual filers may face a cap in the range of $5,000 to $25,000 per year depending on final statutory text; passthrough owners aggregate their allocable shares against the same individual cap. Excess credits may be carried forward for up to five years in most legislative versions.

Key requirements include: (1) the contributing taxpayer must not receive any goods or services in exchange for the contribution (i.e., no quid pro quo); (2) the SGO must be certified or registered under applicable federal or state criteria; (3) the contribution must be made in cash or qualified property during the taxable year for which the credit is claimed; and (4) the taxpayer must obtain written acknowledgment from the SGO confirming the contribution amount and the organization's qualifying status.

SMB owners operating as S corporations, partnerships, or sole proprietors can participate by making direct contributions or by having their passthrough entity contribute on their behalf. Note that because this credit applies primarily to individuals and passthroughs, C corporations are generally not eligible under the framework described. See IRC §170 and relevant legislative guidance for full requirements.
documentation_required: Written acknowledgment from the scholarship granting organization
IRS Form 8283 (for non-cash contributions over $500)
Proof of payment (bank records, cancelled checks, wire transfer confirmations)
Schedule K-1 (for passthrough entity owners)
Evidence of SGO's §501(c)(3) status
Records of any state SGO certification or registration

name: Credit for Employer-Paid Family and Medical Leave
eligibility_text:
Under IRC §45S, eligible employers may claim a general business credit equal to a percentage of wages paid to qualifying employees during any period in which those employees are on family and medical leave. The credit was originally enacted as a temporary provision and has been extended by subsequent legislation; employers should verify current-year applicability.

To qualify, an employer must have a written paid family and medical leave policy that: (1) provides at least two weeks of annual paid family and medical leave to all qualifying full-time employees (prorated for part-time employees); and (2) pays those employees at least 50 percent of their normal wages during the leave period. Employers with fewer than 50 employees are not required to comply with the FMLA itself, but must still maintain a written policy meeting §45S requirements.

A 'qualifying employee' is any employee who has been employed by the employer for at least one year and whose prior-year compensation did not exceed a specified threshold (for recent tax years, approximately $81,000, indexed for inflation — see IRC §45S(c)(2) and annual IRS guidance for the current-year limit).

The credit rate is 12.5 percent of qualifying wages paid during leave when the wage replacement rate equals exactly 50 percent of normal wages. The credit rate increases by 0.25 percentage points for each percentage point by which the wage replacement rate exceeds 50 percent, up to a maximum credit rate of 25 percent. For example, if an employer pays 100 percent of normal wages during leave, the credit rate is 25 percent.

The credit applies only to wages paid for leave taken for reasons that qualify under the Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA), including the birth or adoption of a child, serious health condition of the employee or a family member, qualifying military exigency, or care for a covered servicemember. Leave paid by a state or local government program or required by state or local law is generally excluded from qualifying wages for credit purposes.

The amount of qualifying wages used to compute the credit cannot exceed the employee's normal weekly wage. Employers must reduce their wage deduction by the amount of the credit claimed. The credit is part of the general business credit under IRC §38 and is subject to the general business credit carryback and carryforward rules. Employers should complete IRS Form 8994 to calculate and claim the credit and attach it to their annual return. See IRC §45S and IRS Form 8994 instructions for full details.
documentation_required: Written paid family and medical leave policy
Employee leave records showing qualifying FMLA-reason leave taken
Payroll records documenting wages paid during leave periods
Documentation of each qualifying employee's tenure (1+ year)
Prior-year W-2s or payroll records confirming employee compensation does not exceed the threshold
IRS Form 8994
Completed annual income tax return (Form 1120, 1065, 1120-S, or Schedule C as applicable)

name: Work Opportunity Tax Credit
eligibility_text:
The Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC), authorized under IRC §51, provides a tax credit to employers who hire individuals from one or more designated target groups that have historically faced barriers to employment. The credit is available to all for-profit employers and, in limited circumstances, to certain tax-exempt organizations under IRC §52(c) for hiring qualified veterans. The credit applies to wages paid in the first and, for long-term family assistance recipients, second year of employment.

Target groups under IRC §51(d) include: qualified IV-A recipients, qualified veterans (including those with service-connected disabilities), qualified ex-felons, designated community residents, vocational rehabilitation referrals, qualified summer youth employees, qualified Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) recipients, qualified Supplemental Security Income (SSI) recipients, long-term family assistance recipients, and qualified long-term unemployment recipients.

The general credit equals 40% of first-year qualified first-year wages (up to $6,000 per employee) for employees who work at least 400 hours, yielding a maximum general credit of $2,400 per employee. If the employee works at least 120 but fewer than 400 hours, the credit rate drops to 25%, yielding a maximum of $1,500. For qualified veterans with a service-connected disability who have been unemployed for at least 6 months, the wage cap increases to $24,000, producing a maximum credit of $9,600. For long-term family assistance recipients, wages in the second year (up to $10,000) also qualify, with a 50% credit rate on second-year wages, giving a maximum total credit of $9,000 over two years. Qualified summer youth employees face a $3,000 wage cap and the 40%/25% structure applies.

To claim the credit, employers must obtain certification from the appropriate State Workforce Agency (SWA) that the hired individual is a member of a target group. The employer must submit IRS Form 8850 (Pre-Screening Notice and Certification Request) to the SWA no later than 28 calendar days after the eligible worker's start date. Failure to obtain timely certification generally disqualifies the credit.

The WOTC is a general business credit under IRC §38 and is subject to the tax liability limitation under IRC §38(c). Unused credits may be carried back 1 year and carried forward 20 years. The credit reduces the employer's deduction for wages paid under IRC §280C(a). Wages used for the WOTC cannot be used to calculate other wage-based credits for the same employee. See IRC §51 and §52 for full requirements, including controlled group aggregation rules.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8850 (Pre-Screening Notice and Certification Request) submitted to State Workforce Agency within 28 days of hire
State Workforce Agency (SWA) certification letter confirming target group membership
ETA Form 9061 or 9062 (Individual Characteristics Form or Conditional Certification)
Payroll records documenting wages paid and hours worked per certified employee
Employment start and end dates for each certified hire
IRS Form 5884 filed with annual tax return
Records identifying target group category for each certified employee

name: Credit for Employer-Provided Dependent Care
eligibility_text:
Under IRC §45F, employers may claim a credit for expenses incurred to provide dependent care assistance to employees. The credit is available to any employer — including corporations, partnerships, S corporations, and sole proprietors — that pays or incurs qualifying dependent care expenses during the taxable year.

The credit has two components. First, employers may claim 25% of qualified expenses paid or incurred to acquire, construct, rehabilitate, or expand a facility used to provide dependent care services to employees. Second, employers may claim 10% of amounts paid or incurred to provide or sponsor dependent care services for employees, including payments to a third-party dependent care facility or resource-and-referral services.

The combined credit under §45F is capped at $150,000 per taxable year. Qualifying dependent care facilities must meet all applicable state and local requirements for the operation of a dependent care program. The facility or services must be made available to employees on a basis that does not discriminate in favor of highly compensated employees.

For purposes of the credit, 'dependent care services' generally means care for a qualifying child (as defined under IRC §152(f)(1)) under age 13, or for a dependent or spouse who is physically or mentally incapable of caring for themselves, while the employee is at work.

Any expenses for which the §45F credit is claimed must be reduced from any other deduction or credit otherwise available for the same expenses (no double benefit). The credit is part of the general business credit under IRC §38 and is subject to the general business credit carryback (1 year) and carryforward (20 years) rules.

For a facility placed in service and later ceases to be used for dependent care within 10 years, a recapture rule applies. If the facility is disposed of or ceases to be used as a dependent care facility within 3 years of being placed in service, 100% of the credit is recaptured; between years 3 and 10, a prorated recapture schedule applies under §45F(d).

There are no gross receipts limitations or employee-count caps specified in the statute, making this credit available to employers of any size. Small and mid-size employers with on-site dependent care centers, childcare subsidy programs, or dependent care resource-and-referral contracts are common claimants. See IRC §45F and IRS Form 8882 instructions for full requirements and recapture provisions.
documentation_required: IRS Form 8882 (Credit for Employer-Provided Childcare Facilities and Services)
Records of qualified childcare facility acquisition, construction, or rehabilitation costs
Records of amounts paid to third-party dependent care facilities or resource-and-referral providers
Documentation that the facility meets applicable state and local licensing requirements
Employee utilization records demonstrating nondiscrimination
Lease or ownership records for employer-operated dependent care facilities
Recapture tracking records for facilities placed in service within the prior 10 years
